does: This paper introduces Text Preference Optimization (TPO), a novel alignment framework for text-to-image diffusion models that achieves preference-based optimization without requiring paired image preference data. The core method trains models to prefer matched prompts over synthetically generated mismatched prompts created by perturbing original captions with a language model. By leveraging text-level perturbations rather than image annotations, TPO enables alignment at significantly reduced cost. The approach is agnostic to the underlying diffusion model architecture and compatible with existing preference-based algorithms, demonstrating that text-image alignment can be substantially improved through this synthetic preference construction mechanism.
connects: This work builds on the recent push to align text-to-image diffusion models with human preferences through reinforcement learning. It directly responds to limitations in prior RLHF-based approaches like DPOK and other preference-based methods that require expensive human-annotated paired image comparisons or learned reward models. The paper extends the broader alignment literature from language models (where techniques like DPO have enabled preference optimization without reward models) to the vision domain. It also connects to work on synthetic data generation and prompt engineering, leveraging language models to construct contrastive training signals. The fundamental insight draws from preference learning theory but applies it creatively to the multimodal domain where text can be manipulated more easily than images.
enables: This framework opens several research directions by demonstrating that alignment need not depend on costly image-level human annotations. Future work can explore more sophisticated prompt perturbation strategies beyond simple language model-based approaches, investigate whether synthetic mismatches capture all relevant human preference dimensions, and extend the method to other multimodal generation tasks. The efficiency gains suggest scalable alignment of large diffusion models with minimal annotation overhead, potentially enabling rapid iteration on model improvements. Additionally, this work invites investigation into what properties of text perturbations best drive visual alignment and whether text-level preferences can fully substitute for image-based human feedback in practical deployment scenarios.
